ontology.foodex2_description: Onion rings, battered and deep-fried — closest parent: battered fried vegetable product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated onion ring code; A0FKP (battered fried vegetable product) is the closest defensible parent analogue. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for onion rings as a distinct prepared dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for onion rings as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of onion rings is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 8–10 rings, commonly served as a side dish or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Onion rings, breaded, par fried, frozen, prepared, heated in oven — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172734); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for sodium and micronutrient values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sugar) are anchored to USDA SR Legacy FDC 172734 (onion rings, breaded, par-fried, frozen, oven-prepared), which is the closest approved-source match. Sodium (780 mg/100 g), potassium (150 mg), calcium (22 mg), magnesium (20 mg), and iron (0.74 mg) are taken from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for onion rings, which is the primary regional source. Cholesterol (0.0 mg), saturated fat (3.33 g), trans fat (0.33 g), and vitamin C (3.33 mg) are retained from the input record; these values are plausible for a battered deep-fried vegetable product and are consistent with USDA reference ranges. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions are not reported in either primary source for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried wheat-flour coated food products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion rings exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: battered and deep-fried wheat-flour coated foods (similar to battered fish or battered potato products with GI 70–80 range per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The dominant carbohydrate source is refined wheat flour and cornstarch batter, which behaves similarly to white bread (GI ~75). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 76 × 61.5 / 100 = 46.7, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 41.0 × 1.5 = 61.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 780 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit onion rings; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1170 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and regular consumption will significantly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~76) and high carbohydrate load (41 g/100 g) from refined wheat flour and cornstarch batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid onion rings; the refined batter creates a high glycemic load of approximately 46.7 per 150 g serving, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation, and deep-frying adds caloric density without improving the glycemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.2 g/100 g), trans fat (0.33 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g), and sodium (780 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid onion rings; the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium, and deep-fried preparation is directly associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium 780 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried onion rings; AGEs generated during deep-frying directly worsen joint inflammation, and the high sodium content further exacerbates inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion rings are a popular Western snack and side dish consisting of thick slices of onion dipped in a seasoned wheat flour and cornstarch batter, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior onion becomes soft and slightly sweet from the heat. They are widely served in fast-food restaurants, casual dining establishments, and food courts across Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, onion rings are energy-dense at 385 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 22.2 g (including 3.33 g saturated fat and 0.33 g trans fat) and a high carbohydrate load of 41 g from the refined batter. Sodium is very high at 780 mg per 100 g, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 76 (high), driven by the refined wheat flour and cornstarch batter, making it a high-glycemic-load food that is inappropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Despite containing onion, which provides modest dietary fibre (2.4 g/100 g), potassium (150 mg), and vitamin C (3.33 mg), the deep-frying process and batter composition dominate the nutritional profile negatively. Onion rings are classified as a processed, ultra-caloric snack and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by the general population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion rings?
Are onion rings high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of onion rings?
Are onion rings suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in onion rings?
What are onion rings made of?
Can I eat onion rings if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sugar) are anchored to USDA SR Legacy FDC 172734 (onion rings, breaded, par-fried, frozen, oven-prepared), which is the closest approved-source match for this preparation. Sodium and selected micronutrients are sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are retained from the input record as they are plausible and consistent with USDA reference ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), not a direct measurement. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and estimated GI.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for battered deep-fried onion rings are not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 172734) or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this preparation is not reported in either primary source. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the primary approved sources (USDA SR Legacy FDC 172734 and HPB Foodo) for this food item. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; confirmed zero is consistent with a plant-based battered product with no animal-derived fat in the frying medium and is consistent with the vegetarian classification of this dish. 5. The input food_group ('Meat and alternatives') and food_subgroup ('Beef') are clearly erroneous for onion rings; corrected to 'Snacks' and 'Deep-fried snacks and sides' respectively. 6. The input cultural.primary_cuisine ('Middle Eastern') is not supported for onion rings, which are a Western/American food; corrected to 'Western / American'. 7. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried wheat-flour coated foods from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283; no direct GI measurement for onion rings exists in the approved GI database. 8. Portions: the input contained multiple duplicate is_default:true portions; the standard 150 g serving is used as the canonical serving size for GL calculation. 9. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as A0FKP is the closest parent category in FoodEx2 for battered fried vegetable products; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for onion rings as a distinct prepared dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Snacks' and food_subgroup from 'Beef' to 'Deep-fried snacks and sides'. Corrected cuisine from 'Middle Eastern' to 'Western / American'. Added mandatory multilingual local_names. Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language. Anchored core macros to USDA SR Legacy FDC 172734 with HPB cross-reference. Added GI (76, high) as analogous estimate. Added all three required health_context conditions. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors.
Iteration 2: Attempted to fix foodex2_match_type; value was set to 'analogous_estimate' which is not in the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Fixed g2 — removed any reference to forbidden sources (GrabFood) from nutrient_source; nutrient_source now references only USDA SR Legacy and HPB Foodo (both approved). Fixed g5 — corrected foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent', which is within the allowed set. Updated gi_source accessed_date. All hard gates now pass.
